title: Design Patterns
description: Collection of design pattern examples and variants across languages.
excerpt: Design Patterns Design patterns are reusable solutions to common software design problems. They provide a template for how to solve a problem that can be used in many different situations. This page explores various design patterns, their i
search_text: Design Patterns Design patterns are reusable solutions to common software design problems. They provide a template for how to solve a problem that can be used in many different situations. This page explores various design patterns, their implementations across different programming languages, and modern variants that leverage contemporary language features. Creational patterns Abstract Factory Create families of related objects without specifying concrete classes. Shown as classic factory-method-based examples. Example implementation across language variants. Builder Step-by-step construction of complex objects using a fluent interface. Example implementation across language variants. Factory Method Define an interface for creating objects but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Example implementation across language variants. Prototype Create new objects by copying a prototypical instance, useful for runtime-configurable object creation. Example implementation across language variants. Singleton Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access. Note: Singleton is often considered an anti-pattern in modern software design due to issues with testability and global state, but it's included here for completeness. Example implementation across language variants. Object Pool Manage a pool of reusable objects to improve performance and resource utilization. Example implementation across language variants. Lazy Initialization Delay the creation of an object until it is needed, improving startup performance and resource usage. Example implementation across language variants. Multiton Ensure a class has only a limited number of instances and provide a global point of access to them. Example implementation across language variants. Structural patterns Adapter Maps one interface to another, enabling integration of incompatible APIs. Example implementation across language variants. Bridge Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so they can vary independently. Example implementation across language variants. Composite Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Example implementation across language variants. Decorator Attach additional responsibilities to objects dynamically via lightweight wrappers. Example implementation across language variants. Facade Provide a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. Example implementation across language variants. Flyweight Use sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently. Example implementation across language variants. Proxy Proxy is sometimes categorized as a structural pattern, sometimes as a behavioral pattern. It's included here for simplicity. If we wanted to be more precise, we could split it into two patterns: Proxy (structural) and Virtual Proxy (behavioral). For now, we'll keep it as a single pattern Provide a surrogate or placeholder to control access to another object. Example implementation across language variants. Data Mapper Separate the in-memory objects from the database, allowing for a clean domain model and flexible data access. Example implementation across language variants. Object Adapter Adapter that uses composition to delegate calls to the adaptee, allowing for more flexibility than class adapters. Example implementation across language variants. Class Adapter Adapter that uses multiple inheritance to adapt one interface to another, but is less flexible than object adapters. Example implementation across language variants. Behavioral patterns Chain Of Responsibility Modern implementations using functional handlers (functions/callables): the chain is a flat vector of handlers; no subclass-linked list is required. Example implementation across language variants. Modern variant of the pattern Command Modern implementations using callables (functions/lambdas): commands can be simple callables replacing class hierarchies. Example implementation across language variants. Modern variant of the pattern Observer Modern implementations using callbacks or function references: any callable subscribes directly, removing the Observer interface in many cases. Example implementation across language variants. Modern variant of the pattern State Modern implementations using variants or algebraic data types where available: states are value types and transitions are assignments, avoiding virtual dispatch. Example implementation across language variants. Modern variant of the pattern Strategy Modern implementations using policy-based or function-based approaches: offers both compile-time and runtime selection strategies. Example implementation across language variants. Modern variant of the pattern Template Method Modern implementations using CRTP or higher-order functions provide compile-time polymorphism without virtual calls. search_text: Insight Blocks Showcase This page demonstrates all four insight block types with consistent iconography and styling. Tip Block Pro Tip: Use the Search Feature tip Press / or Ctrl+K to instantly search through all pages in the Atlas. The search ranks results by title relevance, making it easy to find exactly what you need. Tips are highlighted in yellow and use a lightbulb icon to indicate helpful advice and best practices. Warning Block Common Pitfall: Breaking Changes warning When upgrading dependencies, always check the changelog for breaking changes. Test your code thoroughly before deploying to production. Warnings are highlighted in orange and use an alert triangle icon to catch user attention. Important Block Critical: Security Configuration important Never commit secrets or API keys to version control. Always use environment variables or secure secret management tools. This is a critical security requirement. Important blocks are highlighted in red and use a lightning bolt icon for critical information. Takeaway Block Key Takeaway: Consistency is Key takeaway The most important aspect of code design is consistency. Whether you choose tabs or spaces, camelCase or snake_case, make sure the choice is consistent throughout your project. Takeaways are highlighted in purple and use a star icon to emphasize key points and main concepts. Mixed Content with Insights Insight blocks work well alongside other content types: Documentation Best Practice tip Always include examples in your documentation. Code snippets are more valuable than lengthy explanations. Use clear, descriptive variable names Add comments for complex logic Keep functions small and focused Write unit tests for critical paths Testing Requirements important All new features must have corresponding unit tests. Aim for at least 80% code coverage on critical paths.

title: principles
description: Coding principles and best practices for writing clean, efficient, and maintainable code across different programming languages.
excerpt: Coding Principles This page outlines key coding principles and best practices that are essential for writing clean, efficient, and maintainable code across different programming languages. Single Responsibility Principle A class or module s
search_text: Coding Principles This page outlines key coding principles and best practices that are essential for writing clean, efficient, and maintainable code across different programming languages. Single Responsibility Principle A class or module should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one responsibility or job. Open/Closed Principle Software entities (classes, modules, functions) should be open for extension but closed for modification. Liskov Substitution Principle Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without affecting the correctness of the program. Interface Segregation Principle Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use. Instead, many specific interfaces are better than one general interface. Dependency Inversion Principle High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions. Abstractions should not depend on details. Details should depend on abstractions.

title: Course Mode | Learning Pathways
description: Step-by-step guided roadmaps to master programming concepts across multiple languages simultaneously.
excerpt: Welcome to Course Mode The Guided Learning Experience Unlike the Atlas, which serves as a quick reference for syntax, Course Mode is designed for deep, structured learning. Here, we break down complex computer science topics into digestible
search_text: Welcome to Course Mode The Guided Learning Experience Unlike the Atlas, which serves as a quick reference for syntax, Course Mode is designed for deep, structured learning. Here, we break down complex computer science topics into digestible lessons that demonstrate how fundamental logic remains consistent across different programming environments. How Pathways Work Concept-First Education Each course follows a 'Concept-First' approach. Instead of merely memorizing keywords, you learn a core concept—such as Memory Management or Concurrency—and immediately see how that concept is implemented in the languages you have selected in the selector bar. Progressive Difficulty Lessons are arranged in the left sidebar from fundamental to advanced. We recommend following the sequence to build a solid mental model of how software works under the hood. Available Pathways Currently, the following pathways are available for exploration: The Foundations: Data types, variables, and control flow. Systems Thinking: Memory allocation, pointers, and performance. Object Orientation: Classes, inheritance, and polymorphism across languages.

search_text: Exercises This section contains practice problems and coding exercises to reinforce learning. Beginner Exercises A set of exercises designed for beginners to practice basic programming concepts. Hello World Write a program that prints 'Hello, World!' to the console. Sum of Numbers Write a program that calculates the sum of the first n natural numbers. Factorial Calculation Write a program that calculates the factorial of a given number. Prime Number Check Write a program that checks if a given number is prime. Maximum of an Array Write a program that finds the maximum element in an array. Even-Odd Check Determine if a number is even or odd. FizzBuzz Print numbers from 1 to n. For multiples of 3, print 'Fizz' instead of the number, and for multiples of 5, print 'Buzz'. For numbers which are multiples of both 3 and 5, print 'FizzBuzz'. Intermediate Exercises A set of exercises designed for intermediate learners to practice more complex programming concepts. Reverse a String Write a program that reverses a given string. Binary Search Implement the binary search algorithm to find the index of a target value in a sorted array. Merge Sort Implement the merge sort algorithm to sort an array. Anagram Check Write a program that checks if two strings are anagrams of each other. Stack Implementation Implement a stack with basic operations such as push, pop, and peek. Queue Implementation Implement a queue with basic operations such as enqueue, dequeue, and peek. Binary Tree Traversal Implement in-order, pre-order, and post-order traversal of a binary tree. Divide and Conquer: Quick Sort Implement the quick sort algorithm using the divide and conquer approach. Greedy Algorithm: Huffman Coding Implement Huffman coding for data compression using a greedy algorithm. Backtracking: Sudoku Solver Solve a Sudoku puzzle using backtracking. Dynamic Programming: Longest Common Subsequence Find the longest common subsequence of two strings using dynamic programming. Divide and Conquer: Closest Pair of Points Find the closest pair of points in a 2D plane using a divide and conquer algorithm. Greedy Algorithm: Kruskal's Algorithm Implement Kruskal's algorithm to find the minimum spanning tree of a graph. Backtracking: Permutations of a String Generate all permutations of a given string using backtracking. Dynamic Programming: Coin Change Problem Determine the number of ways to make change for a given amount using a set of coin denominations. Divide and Conquer: Matrix Multiplication Implement matrix multiplication using the divide and conquer approach. Backtracking: Combination Sum Find all unique combinations of numbers that sum up to a target value using backtracking. Dynamic Programming: Edit Distance Calculate the edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between two strings using dynamic programming. Divide and Conquer: Count Inversions in an Array Count the number of inversions in an array using a divide and conquer algorithm. Greedy Algorithm: Job Scheduling Problem Schedule jobs to maximize profit using a greedy algorithm. Backtracking: Subset Sum Problem Determine if there is a subset of a given set of numbers that sums up to a target value using backtracking. Dynamic Programming: Longest Increasing Subsequence Find the length of the longest increasing subsequence in an array using dynamic programming. Divide and Conquer: Count Occurrences in a Sorted Array Count the number of occurrences of a target value in a sorted array using a divide and conquer algorithm. Greedy Algorithm: Fractional Knapsack Problem Solve the fractional knapsack problem using a greedy algorithm. Dynamic Programming: Palindrome Partitioning Partition a string into all possible palindrome partitions using dynamic programming. Divide and Conquer: Majority Element Find the majority element in an array using a divide and conquer algorithm. Greedy Algorithm: Huffman Coding Implement Huffman coding for data compression using a greedy algorithm. Backtracking: Word Search Determine if a given word exists in a 2D grid of characters using backtracking. Linked List Implementation Implement a singly linked list with basic operations such as insertion, deletion, and traversal. Array Reverse Reverse an array in place. Fibonacci Sequence Generate the Fibonacci sequence up to a given number. Palindrome Check Check if a string is a palindrome. Advanced Exercises Graph Traversal: Depth-First Search and Breadth-First Search Implement depth-first search (DFS) and breadth-first search (BFS) algorithms for graph traversal. Dijkstra's Algorithm for Shortest Path Implement Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest path in a weighted graph. Dynamic Programming: Knapsack Problem Solve the 0/1 knapsack problem using dynamic programming. Backtracking: N-Queens Problem Solve the N-Queens problem using backtracking. Greedy Algorithm: Activity Selection Problem Solve the activity selection problem using a greedy algorithm.

title: Welcome | Polyglot Guide
excerpt: Welcome to the Polyglot Guide | Meta Mode Guide Welcome to Meta Mode — the centralized documentation for the framework and build-system features. This mode is dedicated to explaining the inner workings of the Polyglot Dev Atlas. It serves a
search_text: Welcome to the Polyglot Guide | Meta Mode Guide Welcome to Meta Mode — the centralized documentation for the framework and build-system features. This mode is dedicated to explaining the inner workings of the Polyglot Dev Atlas. It serves as both a manual and a live testing environment for new engine features. This entire guide is generated from JSON files located in content/pages/meta/. This page uses meta.css for styling with "style": "meta-style". info Getting Started This guide explains how to use Meta Mode and the available utilities. Usage Use the mode switcher to enter Meta mode. Pages in this mode live under content/pages/meta/. The Build Pipeline The engine uses a 4-stage process to transform your data into a high-performance static site: Registry Scan: Python maps all JSON files to build the navigation sidebars. Schema Parsing: The recursive renderer translates JSON objects into HTML tags. Asset Syncing: CSS, JS, and Syntax Highlighting files are mirrored to the distribution folder. Persistence Injection: LocalStorage logic is baked into the headers to prevent UI flashing. Core Technologies The Atlas is designed to be zero-dependency on the client side, utilizing standard modern web APIs: Python 3: The static site generator core. CSS Grid & Variables: Powering the geometric design system and color palettes. Intersection Observer: Driving the high-performance Scroll-Spy sidebar. Highlight.js: Handling multi-language syntax highlighting.

title: Hierarchical Structure
excerpt: Nesting & Recursive Blocks The Atlas engine is built on a recursive rendering logic. A blocks element can contain an array of other blocks, allowing you to create deeply nested documentation hierarchies. 1. Smart H
search_text: Nesting & Recursive Blocks The Atlas engine is built on a recursive rendering logic. A blocks element can contain an array of other blocks, allowing you to create deeply nested documentation hierarchies. 1. Smart Heading Logic To maintain semantic structure without manual tagging, use type: 'title'. The engine automatically calculates the correct heading level (h2 through h6) based on the nesting depth. Example: This is an H3 Because this block is inside another block, its title automatically shrank from H2 to H3. 2. Automatic Visibility Depth To prevent 'Information Overload', the engine implements a default visibility rule: The 2-Level Rule. Levels 1 & 2: Expanded by default (depth 2 and 3). Levels 3+: Automatically collapsed on page load (depth 4+). Any block at Level 3 or deeper will appear with the is-collapsed state unless you manually override it. warning 3. Manual Toggles You can bypass the automatic visibility logic by using the collapsed property in the JSON. 'collapsed': true — Forces a block to start hidden. 'collapsed': false — Forces a deep block to start visible.

title: Content Element Library
excerpt: Element Showcase The engine processes a specific set of JSON objects. Each type maps to a semantic HTML structure designed for technical documentation. Typography & Headings Standard paragraphs use type: 'text'. Headings use
search_text:
Element Showcase The engine processes a specific set of JSON objects. Each type maps to a semantic HTML structure designed for technical documentation. Typography & Headings Standard paragraphs use type: 'text'. Headings use type: 'title'. Titles are 'smart'—their size scales based on how many blocks deep they are buried. Callouts & Notes Notes are used to highlight specific information. They support different variants via the variant key. This is an info variant. Useful for general tips. info This is a warning variant. Use this to highlight anti-patterns or dangers. warning This is an error variant. Use this to highlight critical issues or common mistakes. error Formatting Notes The content inside notes can be formatted with the same JSON structure as regular pages. This allows you to include lists, code blocks, and even tables inside notes for maximum clarity. prefer inline <code>…</code> for short labels. For multi-line code use a separate pre block outside the note, or include a small pre inside the note — the renderer supports it but keep tables and large blocks outside notes for best layout. Avoid raw block-level HTML inside notes unless necessary. Examples (copyable) Basic — Inline
Use inline code for short labels: <code>npm install</code>. info Small code inside a note
const greet = (name) => `Hello ${name}`; console.log(greet('World'));

keep full examples outside notes for better layout. Example JSON snippet to reference a code block:

error Lists Lists support both unordered (bullet) and ordered (numbered) formats. Bullet item one Bullet item two First steps Second steps Code Comparison Grid The code type is the heart of the Atlas. It looks for files in content/snippets/ that match the provided name. design_patterns_singleton The grid above automatically responds to the primary/secondary selection logic and applies theme-aware syntax highlighting. info Images Images use the image type and support captions. title: Styling & Customization
excerpt: The Three-Tier Style System The Polyglot Atlas v2 separates structure from aesthetics. You can control the 'feel' of the interface through three different layers of customization. 1. Global Geometric Styles The Style Picker in the navbar co
search_text: The Three-Tier Style System The Polyglot Atlas v2 separates structure from aesthetics. You can control the 'feel' of the interface through three different layers of customization. 1. Global Geometric Styles The Style Picker in the navbar controls the physical properties of all components globally. These are driven by CSS variables like --radius and --pad. ⬚ Sharp: Zero border-radius, thin borders, compact padding. Best for a terminal/IDE look. ◯ Round: Standard 12px radius, soft shadows. The default modern experience. ☁ Airy: 24px radius, double padding, and heavy shadows. Designed for high-readability focus. 2. Page Presets (Inheritance) As seen in this guide, setting a preset at the top of the JSON file changes the default appearance of all nested content. Setting 'style': 'text_style' in the root JSON object makes all blocks render without borders, resembling a documentation article. info 3. Local Block Overrides Individual blocks can break away from the page preset by defining their own style property. I am an Override Even though this page uses text_style, this specific block has its style set to empty (default), so it appears as a standard bordered card. 4. Information Density Independent of the visual style, the Detailed / Compact toggle in the right sidebar controls how many levels of the Table of Contents are visible. Detailed: Shows every nested sub-topic in the sidebar. Compact: Hides everything below Level 1 for a cleaner navigation experience.
